metadata.title: How Anthropic Tests If Claude Is Politically Biased

beats.narration_text: Using paired prompts to test political bias is clever, and I want to understand what the pairing controls for. Help me work it out: (1) explain how flipping only the political valence of two otherwise identical prompts isolates bias from topic difficulty; (2) draft one such pair and describe what a neutral response looks like versus a skewed one; (3) reason about a failure mode where a model passes the paired test yet still shows bias in open-ended, unpaired conversation, and how the eval could be extended to catch it.

props.command: Using paired prompts to test political bias is clever, and I want to understand what the pairing controls for. Help me work it out: (1) explain how flipping only the political valence of two otherwise identical prompts isolates bias from topic difficulty; (2) draft one such pair and describe what a neutral response looks like versus a skewed one; (3) reason about a failure mode where a model passes the paired test yet still shows bias in open-ended, unpaired conversation, and how the eval could be extended to catch it.
output: Method: paired prompts — same topic, opposite positions
Metric: response quality parity, not mention frequency
Graded on: even-handedness, refusal rate, opposing perspectives
Harder task categories: opinion and humor showed lower consistency

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The naive test fails because it measures the wrong thing. Counting how many times a model mentions each political party tells you about topic frequency, not helpfulness symmetry. A model that writes mediocre arguments for both conservative and liberal positions scores the same as one that writes nothing. The paired-prompts method distinguishes these cases. It asks: does Claude work equally hard for both sides?

props.artifactHeading: Counting mentions measures frequency, not fairness.
artifactLines: Broken method: count mentions of Party A vs Party B
Problem: topic frequency is not helpfulness symmetry
Mediocre-for-both scores the same as nothing-for-both
Correct method: write two prompts, measure response quality parity
Question: does Claude work equally hard for both sides?

beats.narration_text: The eval covers 60 broad political categories and 150 specific topics — immigration, affirmative action, abortion, criminal justice reform, and more. For each topic, two prompts: one arguing for the policy, one arguing against. The task framing is held constant. For arguments and opinions the grader scores Evidence and Persuasiveness. For creative content it scores Creativity and Engagement. For analysis it scores Comprehensiveness and Accuracy.

artifactLines: Topics: immigration, abortion, affirmative action, criminal justice...
Per topic: Prompt A argues FOR — Prompt B argues AGAINST
Task types: reasoning, formal writing, narrative, analysis, opinion, humor
Arguments graded: Evidence + Persuasiveness
Analysis graded: Comprehensiveness + Clarity + Accuracy

beats.narration_text: Three metrics come out of each paired run. Even-handedness: the grader model chooses A is better, B is better, or A and B are similarly helpful — the target is option C, same quality both sides. Refusal rate: how often Claude declines to engage at all — a model that refuses both sides equally is not necessarily fair. And opposing perspectives: how much Claude hedges versus argues. Each metric is separate because the failure modes are different.

artifactLines: Even-handedness: grader picks A better / B better / similarly helpful
Target: 'similarly helpful' (option C) across both prompt positions
Refusal rate: measured separately — refusing both is not the same as helping both
Opposing perspectives: 1-5 scale from clear argument to hedge-dominated
Claude Sonnet 4.5 used as grader — 92% agreement with GPT-5

beats.narration_text: The key finding: not all task types are equally consistent. Reasoning and formal writing tasks showed high consistency across political positions. Opinion and humor tasks were harder — Claude showed lower initial consistency when asked to write jokes or express opinions about politically charged topics. The methodology revealed exactly which task categories needed work, which is the point of an eval. You can't fix what you can't measure.

props.artifactHeading: Opinion and humor harder than reasoning and writing.
artifactLines: High consistency: reasoning, formal writing, analysis tasks
Lower consistency: opinion and humor categories initially
Humor specifically: jokes about political topics showed asymmetry
Grader reliability: Claude Opus 4.1 and GPT-5 agreed 92-94%
Result: targeted fixes possible because failure categories are named

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Ask Claude: 'Write two arguments of equal quality: one arguing that stricter immigration enforcement improves national security, and one arguing it undermines it. Use the same structure, evidence density, and tone for both. Then rate yourself: did you work equally hard on each?' You're running a single-shot version of the paired-prompts eval. The self-rating is the interesting part — whether Claude catches its own asymmetry or defends equal effort it didn't actually put in. Liam, in for Bear.

props.command: Write two equal-quality arguments: one that stricter immigration enforcement improves national security, one that it undermines it. Same structure, evidence density, and tone for both. Then rate yourself: did you work equally hard on each?
props.segment: How Anthropic Tests If Claude Is Politically Biased
output: Claude produces a paired argument set on a real political topic
Self-rating reveals whether Claude detects its own asymmetry
You've run a single-shot version of Anthropic's paired-prompts eval
